---
title: ""
page-layout: full
toc: false
css: styles.scss
---

::: {.hero}

::: {.hero-text}
# Sequence analysis<br>for social science.

::: {.lead}
A modern Python library for categorical sequence analysis.
Built on **polars** and **plotnine** — no pandas, no matplotlib API.
Designed for life-course and social-science researchers.
:::

::: {.cta-group}
<a href="getting-started/installation.html" class="btn-primary-custom">Get Started →</a>
<a href="api/index.html" class="btn-secondary-custom">API Reference</a>
:::
:::

::: {.hero-code}
```python
from yasqat import Alphabet, SequencePool
from yasqat.metrics.optimal_matching import OptimalMatchingMetric
from yasqat.clustering import pam_clustering
from yasqat.visualization import index_plot

# Define labour-market states
alpha = Alphabet(
    states=["Employed", "Unemployed", "Education", "Inactive"],
    colors=["#0f766e", "#dc2626", "#d97706", "#71717a"],
)

# Build a pool from your data
pool = SequencePool.from_dataframe(df, alphabet=alpha)

# Compute pairwise distances
dm = OptimalMatchingMetric().pairwise(pool)

# Cluster and visualise
labels = pam_clustering(dm, k=4).labels
index_plot(pool, group=labels).draw()
```
:::

:::

::: {.feature-grid}

::: {.feature}
::: {.feature-label}
Polars-native
:::
### Fast by design

No pandas dependency. All data operations run through polars — columnar,
lazy, and fast even on large panel datasets with thousands of sequences.

```python
# Returns a polars DataFrame
pool.state_distribution()
```
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.feature-label}
Distance metrics
:::
### Seven metrics included

Optimal Matching, Hamming, LCS, DTW, and more. Every metric outputs a
`DistanceMatrix` — a typed numpy wrapper compatible with all clustering functions.

```python
dm = HammingMetric().pairwise(pool)
dm.values   # numpy ndarray, shape (n, n)
```
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.feature-label}
TraMineR-compatible
:::
### Algorithms you already know

Algorithm correctness cross-referenced against TraMineR (R) and TanaT (Python).
Naming conventions follow TraMineR so the transition from R is immediate.

```python
turbulence(pool)        # Brzinsky-Fay
discrepancy_analysis()  # pseudo-R²
```
:::

::: {.feature}
::: {.feature-label}
Visualization
:::
### Nine plot types, one grammar

Index plots, timelines, modal states, parallel coordinates — all return
composable `plotnine.ggplot` objects. Compose with `+` exactly like ggplot2 in R.

```python
p = index_plot(pool)
p + theme_bw()   # fully composable
```
:::

:::
